runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012) - 'Bounds on Elasticities With Optimization Frictions', Review of Economic Studies, reporting compensated elasticities in the 0.2–0.5 range for prime-age workers
CBO (2012) - 'Labor Supply and Fiscal Policy', noting primary-earner elasticities around 0.1–0.3
Keane (2011) - 'Labor Supply and Taxes: A Survey', Journal of Economic Literature, summarizing substitution elasticities for primary earners typically 0.1–0.4
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically male heads of household or the main earner in a couple) are generally found to have relatively inelastic labor supply compared to secondary earners. The literature consensus places compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners in the range of roughly 0.1 to 0.4, with point estimates often around 0.2–0.3. Decile 7 represents upper-middle income earners who face moderate effective marginal tax rates under current U.S. law. At this income level, workers are past the kink points of many phase-outs but still subject to standard marginal rates, suggesting moderate responsiveness. I center my estimate at 0.25 as a reasonable middle-of-the-road substitution elasticity for this group, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.10 to 0.45.

The literature consensus places compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners in the range of roughly 0.1 to 0.4, with point estimates often around 0.2\u20130.3. Decile 7 represents upper-middle income earners who face moderate effective marginal tax rates under current U.S. law. At this income level, workers are past the kink points of many phase-outs but still subject to standard marginal rates, suggesting moderate responsiveness. I center my estimate at 0.25 as a reasonable middle-of-the-road substitution elasticity for this group, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.10 to 0.45."}

citations: Chetty (2012, Annual Review of Economics) – survey of labor supply elasticities finding compensated elasticities for primary earners typically in the 0.1–0.4 range

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 7 are solidly middle-to-upper-middle income, where labor supply tends to be relatively inelastic compared to secondary earners or low-income workers. The standard range used in U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations for primary-earner compensated (substitution) elasticities is roughly 0.1–0.4. Decile 7 primary earners are not at the extremes of the income distribution, so I center my belief near 0.25, consistent with CBO-style simulations and the Chetty (2012) consensus. Uncertainty spans roughly 0.05 to 0.50, reflecting genuine disagreement in the literature and model-specific calibration choices.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012, Annual Review of Economics) — meta-analysis of labor supply elasticities, finding compensated elasticities for primary earners around 0.1–0.3

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 7 (roughly the 60th–70th percentile of earnings) are solidly middle-to-upper-middle income. The empirical literature consistently finds that primary (typically male, full-time) earners have relatively inelastic labor supply on the extensive and intensive margins. Compensated (substitution) elasticities for this group tend to range from about 0.1 to 0.3 in mainstream U.S. microsimulation frameworks. Decile 7 earners are past the steepest phase-out regions of EITC but face moderate marginal rates, suggesting a modest substitution response. I center my belief at 0.20 with meaningful uncertainty, reflecting variation across models and parameterizations.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012, Annual Review of Economics) – survey of labor supply elasticities distinguishing substitution and income effects, suggesting compensated elasticities for primary earners cluster around 0.1–0.5

Keane (2011, Journal of Economic Literature) – review noting male prime-age worker substitution elasticities typically 0.1–0.3, somewhat higher for upper-middle earners
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 7 (upper-middle income, roughly $70k–$90k household earnings range) face moderate effective marginal tax rates and are generally treated in the literature as having compensated (substitution) labor supply elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.4. The consensus from empirical and simulation studies places the substitution elasticity for this group near 0.25, reflecting that these workers are largely full-time employees with limited short-run hours flexibility but non-trivial longer-run margins. Decile 7 is above median but not in the top brackets, so the elasticity is not expected to deviate strongly from the central estimates used in standard U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., CBO's 0.25 benchmark for primary earners). Uncertainty spans roughly 0.10 to 0.45 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

The literature consistently finds that intensive-margin (substitution) elasticities for prime-age primary earners are modest, generally in the 0.1–0.5 range, with a central tendency near 0.2–0.3. Decile 7 sits above median income, where effective marginal tax rates are somewhat elevated but workers are not at the very top; this tends to produce slightly higher elasticity responses than at the median but lower than at the very top. In U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations (e.g., CBO-style models), primary-earner substitution elasticities in this decile are typically calibrated around 0.2–0.3. I center my belief at 0.25 with moderate uncertainty, reflecting both parameter uncertainty and variation across model specifications.

Do not use tools, files, the web, code, or external resources.

- Name: Primary-earner substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit simulation, decile 7

- Target interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model
- Population/context: Primary earners in earnings decile 7 in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation
